content.problemStatement:
A **peak element** in an array is an element that is **strictly greater** than its adjacent elements.

You are provided with a **0-based index integer array** numsnumsnums of size NNN. Your task is to identify the index of **any one peak element** in the array and return it. If multiple peak elements exist, returning the index of any one of them is acceptable.

For simplicity, assume that the elements just outside the boundaries of the array are considered to be negative infinity. That is:


arr[−1]=−∞arr[-1] = -∞arr[−1]=−∞
arr[arr.length]=−∞arr[arr.length] = -∞arr[arr.length]=−∞
Your solution **must have a time complexity of O(log N).**

Function DeclarationFunction NamefindPeakElementfindPeakElementfindPeakElement – This function finds the index of a peak element in the array.

Parameters
numsnumsnums : A 0-based indexed array of integers where adjacent elements are not equal.
Return Value
Returns a single integer — the **index of any peak element**.
Constraints
1≤T≤10001 \leq T \leq 10001≤T≤1000
1≤N≤10001 \leq N \leq 10001≤N≤1000
−231≤nums[i]≤231−1−2^\text{31} \leq nums[i] \leq 2^\text{31} − 1−231≤nums[i]≤231−1
nums[i]≠nums[i+1]nums[i] ≠ nums[i + 1]nums[i]=nums[i+1] for all valid indices iii
Time complexity must be **O(log n)**

Input Format

The first line contains the number of test cases TTT 

Each test case contains:

The first line contains a single integer NNN — the size of the array.
The second line contains NNN space-separated integers representing the array elements.




Output Format

Print a single integer — the index of a peak element.
constraints: nums[i] != nums[i+1] for all valid i
content.inputFormat: First line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case: first line contains integer N, the size of the array. Second line contains N space‑separated integers representing the array. All indices are 0‑based.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing the 0‑based index of any peak element in the given array.

sampleTestCases.explanation: First array: 10 at index 1 is a peak (10 > 5 and 10 > 7). Second array: 6 at index 3 is a peak (6 > 3 and 6 > 1). The binary‑search may also return other valid peaks, but the shown indices satisfy the requirement.
The array is strictly increasing, so the last element (5) is a peak because the virtual element after it is -∞.
The array is strictly decreasing, so the first element (5) is a peak because the virtual element before it is -∞.

solution.approach: The problem can be solved with a classic binary‑search on the array. At any position mid, compare nums[mid] with its right neighbour nums[mid+1]. If nums[mid] > nums[mid+1] the peak lies on the left side (including mid) because the left side cannot be strictly increasing forever due to the -∞ boundary. Otherwise the peak lies on the right side (mid+1 onward). Repeating this shrinks the interval until left == right, which is a peak index. This runs in O(log N) time and O(1) extra space.
solution.timeComplexity: O(log N) per test case, O(T log N) overall.
